APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The paper addresses event coreference (event linking) using convolutional neural sentential features and pairwise similarity models and evaluates on standard corpora (ACE 2005 and ACE++), which squarely fits top-tier NLP venues focused on information extraction and coreference (see Sec. 4 Experimental setting and Sec. 5 Evaluation). It uses methods and evaluation practices expected at these venues (CNNs, pre-trained embeddings, TensorFlow/Adam, hyperparameter search, comparisons to prior work such as Chen & Ji (2009), Cybulska & Vossen (2015) and reports state-of-the-art results), and is positioned within the event-coreference literature (Sec. 6 Related work), making it a strong fit for a top NLP/ML conference.

IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles an important problem—within-document event linking/coreference—which is central to producing coherent document-level IE (Section 1 example and task description). By showing a model that “does not rely on such features but instead utilizes sentential features coming from convolutional neural networks,” it offers a practical, domain-agnostic alternative to KB-heavy systems (Related work / Conclusion). The authors report state-of-the-art performance “on two datasets, one of which is publicly available,” suggesting the approach is likely to be adopted and built upon; while impactful, the advance is an applied methodological improvement rather than a transformative theoretical shift.

REPLICABILITY.justification: Partially reproducible: the paper gives many implementation details (TensorFlow v0.6 implementation, model architecture, loss, Adam optimizer, Ww initialized with Mikolov embeddings, training for 2000 epochs, data splits and a link to ACEvalid/ACEtest), but crucial specifics are missing from the provided text — the final hyperparameter values are only said to be in Table 2 (not included), Adam and other hyperparameter settings are not listed, the ACE++ document list and annotation procedure are not provided, preprocessing/tokenization and exact feature extraction steps are underspecified, evaluation metrics/protocols are not explicitly defined here, and no code release is given. These omissions prevent full reproduction without additional information.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper runs experiments on standard ACE and a larger ACE++ split, reports comparisons to state-of-the-art, and explicitly performs hyper-parameter search (180 random points + line search) and model-variant comparisons (e.g., activation functions, pooling, use of pairwise/lexical features) which indicates solid empirical coverage. It also states that ablation-style comparisons were used to assess feature-class impact and includes an error analysis. Related work coverage is broad and fair. Reproducibility is only partial from the provided text: training details (TensorFlow, Adam, 2000 epochs, pre-trained embeddings) are given and dataset splits are described, but final hyperparameters are only referenced in unseen tables, ACE++ release is unclear, and code/configuration are not provided, hence minor gaps remain.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is largely well-organized and follows a logical flow (problem definition, model, learning, experiments, related work), with concrete examples (the Barclays example) that help illustrate the task. The main contribution — a CNN-based sentential-feature model for within-document event linking and its empirical success — is stated in the summary/introduction text, though an explicit bullet of contributions earlier would improve immediacy. Notation is generally consistent and equations (4–6) and the parameter set θ are defined, but a few concatenated vectors (vsent+lex+pairw) could be spelled out more explicitly. Figures/tables and the included algorithm snippet are informative, but several references to figures/tables in the text are not shown here; writing is clear overall with minor formatting/hyphenation and abbreviation issues (e.g., “wrt.”, broken hyphenation).

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The model equations and architecture descriptions are internally consistent and plausible (Eqs. 4–6, logistic output and training with Adam), so there are no obvious mathematical errors. However, key empirical claims (e.g. "state-of-the-art" on ACE and ACE++) are asserted but no quantitative results, baselines, metrics or significance tests are shown in the provided text, and the reproducibility/quality of the constructed ACE++ corpus is not documented. There are also methodological gaps left unaddressed here (choice of 0.5 threshold and transitive closure for clustering, handling of class imbalance in pairwise training), so while the approach looks reasonable, the provided excerpt lacks sufficient experimental evidence to fully support the main claims.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper states it compares against state-of-the-art systems on ACE (Section 5) and discusses many relevant prior approaches (e.g., Chen & Ji 2009; Lee et al. 2012; Cybulska & Vossen 2015; Araki & Mitamura 2015) in Related Work. It also highlights methodological differences (it does not rely on KB features whereas prior systems do). However, the provided text does not show the detailed experimental baselines, numeric comparisons, or explicit acknowledgements when prior methods perform comparably, so while comparisons appear appropriate they lack some reported detail in the excerpt.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper applies convolutional neural networks to produce sentential latent features for within-document event linking and explicitly argues against reliance on external KB features (see end of abstract/intro). It also proposes a particular similarity computation (square non-linearity) and combines CNN-derived representations with lexical and pairwise features for scoring. These contributions are a clear, well-differentiated adaptation relative to prior event-coreference work (e.g., Chen & Ji 2009; Cybulska & Vossen 2015; Lee et al. 2012), but amount to an incremental/engineering advance—bringing established CNN sentence representations and a learned similarity function to a well-studied problem—rather than a fundamentally new method or problem formulation.
